notes_md:
**lạ** = strange, unfamiliar, not what you’re used to. It’s about something feeling new, different, or unexpected.

It’s an adjective, so it describes people, things, places, smells, feelings, etc. Often used after verbs like *thấy*, *cảm thấy*, *nghe* to talk about your impression.

It can also mean “unfamiliar” (opposite of **quen** = familiar/used to).  
Stronger version: **kỳ lạ** = weird, odd (more dramatic than *lạ*).

definitions.es: Desconocido o extraño; no conocido o reconocido.
definitions.vi: Không quen thuộc hoặc kỳ lạ; không được biết đến hoặc công nhận.
definitions.en: Unfamiliar or strange; not known or recognized.

exampleSentencesTranslated: I find this dish quite strange compared to what I have tried before.
I met a very strange person in the park yesterday.
I feel that the atmosphere in this new city is really strange.
notes:
lạ = strange, unfamiliar, not what you’re used to. It’s about something feeling new, different, or unexpected.

It’s an adjective, so it describes people, things, places, smells, feelings, etc. Often used after verbs like thấy, cảm thấy, nghe to talk about your impression.

It can also mean “unfamiliar” (opposite of quen = familiar/used to).  
Stronger version: kỳ lạ = weird, odd (more dramatic than lạ).

translation.en: This is really strange to me.

exampleSentences: Anh thấy món ăn này khá lạ so với những gì anh từng thử.
Em gặp một người rất lạ ở công viên hôm qua.
Chị cảm thấy không khí ở thành phố mới này thật lạ.
